title: Professional Mastery
description: Achieve professional-level expertise in designing, optimizing, and governing enterprise-scale AI/HPC platforms with advanced strategies for multi-cloud, security, and performance.

lessons.title: Multi-Cluster & Hybrid Cloud
objectives: Understand Kubernetes cluster federation and multi-cluster management patterns
Design hybrid cloud architectures that burst from on-prem HPC to public cloud
Implement workload placement policies across heterogeneous clusters
Compare multi-cloud strategies and their trade-offs for AI/HPC workloads
lessons.content_md:
# Multi-Cluster & Hybrid Cloud for AI/HPC

Modern AI/HPC organizations rarely operate a single cluster. As GPU demand fluctuates and
models grow larger, the ability to federate workloads across on-premises data centers and
multiple cloud providers becomes a critical capability. This lesson covers the architecture
patterns, tooling, and operational strategies for multi-cluster and hybrid cloud deployments.

## Why Multi-Cluster?

A single Kubernetes cluster has practical limits — the upstream recommendation is roughly
5,000 nodes and 150,000 pods. Large-scale training jobs, combined with inference serving
and development workloads, often push organizations beyond this boundary. Additionally,
geographic distribution, blast-radius isolation, and regulatory requirements drive the need
for multiple clusters.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   Control Plane (Hub)                       │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────┐   │
│  │ Admiralty │  │ Cluster API  │  │ Argo CD / Fleet     │   │
│  │ Scheduler │  │  Management  │  │ GitOps Sync         │   │
│  └────┬─────┘  └──────┬───────┘  └──────────┬──────────┘   │
└───────┼───────────────┼──────────────────────┼─────────────┘
        │               │                      │
  ┌─────▼─────┐  ┌─────▼─────┐  ┌─────────────▼──────────┐
  │ On-Prem   │  │  AWS EKS  │  │    GCP GKE Cluster     │
  │ HPC (A100)│  │  (p5.48xl)│  │    (A3 / TPU v5p)      │
  │ InfiniBand│  │  EFA      │  │    GPUDirect-TCPXO     │
  └───────────┘  └───────────┘  └────────────────────────┘
```

## Kubernetes Federation Approaches

There are several approaches to federating Kubernetes clusters for HPC workloads:

**1. KubeFed (Kubernetes Federation v2):** Propagates resource definitions across clusters
using federated types. While mature, it adds operational complexity and is best suited for
uniform workloads rather than heterogeneous GPU scheduling.

**2. Admiralty / Virtual Kubelet:** These tools create virtual nodes that represent remote
clusters. A pod scheduled on a virtual node is transparently dispatched to the target
cluster. This is powerful for cloud-bursting:

```yaml
# Admiralty multi-cluster scheduling source config
apiVersion: multicluster.admiralty.io/v1alpha1
kind: ClusterSource
metadata:
  name: aws-burst-cluster
spec:
  serviceAccount:
    import:
      namespace: admiralty
      name: aws-burst-sa
---
apiVersion: multicluster.admiralty.io/v1alpha1
kind: ClusterTarget
metadata:
  name: aws-burst-cluster
spec:
  self: false
  serviceAccount:
    import:
      namespace: admiralty
      name: aws-burst-sa
```

**3. Rancher Fleet / Argo CD ApplicationSets:** GitOps-driven approaches that render
manifests per-cluster from a single source of truth. Fleet's `BundleDeployment` model
allows cluster-specific overlays:

```yaml
# Fleet GitRepo targeting GPU clusters
apiVersion: fleet.cattle.io/v1alpha1
kind: GitRepo
metadata:
  name: ai-platform-manifests
  namespace: fleet-default
spec:
  repo: https://github.com/org/ai-platform
  branch: main
  paths:
    - overlays/production
  targets:
    - name: gpu-clusters
      clusterSelector:
        matchLabels:
          gpu-type: nvidia-a100
    - name: cloud-burst
      clusterSelector:
        matchLabels:
          provider: aws
```

## Cloud Bursting from On-Prem HPC

Cloud bursting lets on-premises clusters offload jobs to cloud when local capacity is
exhausted. The key design considerations are:

- **Data Gravity:** Large datasets (multi-TB) take hours to transfer. Prefer cloud object
  stores with pre-staged data or use parallel file system bridges (e.g., Weka, VAST with
  cloud tiering).
- **Network Topology:** On-prem InfiniBand (200-400 Gb/s) vastly outperforms cloud
  interconnects. Schedule tightly-coupled distributed training on-prem; embarrassingly
  parallel or inference workloads are ideal burst candidates.
- **Identity Federation:** Use OIDC federation between on-prem identity providers and cloud
  IAM to avoid managing static credentials.

A typical burst controller watches pending pod queue depth and provisions cloud node pools:

```python
# Simplified cloud-burst controller logic
import kubernetes
from cloud_provider import provision_gpu_nodes

def check_burst_needed(core_api, threshold=10, namespace="training"):
    pending = core_api.list_namespaced_pod(
        namespace=namespace,
        field_selector="status.phase=Pending"
    )
    gpu_pending = [
        p for p in pending.items
        if any(
            c.resources.requests.get("nvidia.com/gpu")
            for c in p.spec.containers
        )
    ]
    if len(gpu_pending) > threshold:
        needed_gpus = sum(
            int(c.resources.requests.get("nvidia.com/gpu", 0))
            for p in gpu_pending
            for c in p.spec.containers
        )
        provision_gpu_nodes(
            count=needed_gpus // 8,  # 8-GPU nodes
            instance_type="p5.48xlarge",
            max_nodes=20,
            spot=True
        )
        return True
    return False
```

## Multi-Cloud Strategy

Running across AWS, GCP, and Azure simultaneously is complex but provides leverage in GPU
availability negotiations and resilience. Key patterns include:

- **Active-Active:** All clouds serve production workloads. Requires consistent networking
  (e.g., Aviatrix, Tailscale mesh) and unified observability (Datadog, Grafana Cloud).
- **Active-Passive:** One cloud is primary; others serve as DR or burst targets.
- **Workload-Specific:** Use each cloud for its strengths — GCP for TPUs, AWS for largest
  GPU selection, Azure for Microsoft ecosystem integration.

Regardless of strategy, abstract cloud-specific resources behind Kubernetes APIs. Use
Crossplane or Terraform with Atlantis for provisioning, and ensure training scripts
reference generic device ordinals (`cuda:0`) rather than cloud-specific accelerator names.

exercises.title: Cloud Bursting Best Practices
exercises.description: Which type of AI/HPC workload is the BEST candidate for cloud bursting from an on-premises cluster?
options: Tightly-coupled distributed training with NCCL all-reduce across 256 GPUs
Embarrassingly parallel hyperparameter sweep across independent single-GPU jobs
Real-time inference serving with strict sub-10ms latency SLAs
Interactive Jupyter notebook sessions for data exploration
exercises.correct_answer: Embarrassingly parallel hyperparameter sweep across independent single-GPU jobs
hints: Consider which workloads are most sensitive to inter-node network latency
Cloud interconnects (EFA, GPUDirect-TCPX) are fast but still slower than on-prem InfiniBand
Embarrassingly parallel means each job is independent — no cross-node communication needed
exercises.solution: Embarrassingly parallel hyperparameter sweeps are ideal cloud burst candidates because each trial runs independently on a single GPU with no inter-node communication. This eliminates sensitivity to cloud network latency. Tightly-coupled distributed training requires high-bandwidth, low-latency interconnects (InfiniBand) best served on-prem. Real-time inference needs stable, predictable infrastructure close to users. Notebooks are interactive and latency-sensitive.

exercises.title: Implement Multi-Cluster Workload Distribution
exercises.description:
Design and implement a multi-cluster workload distribution strategy:

1. Write a Fleet GitRepo manifest that targets three cluster types: on-prem GPU,
   cloud GPU, and cloud CPU — each with appropriate label selectors.
2. Create a Python script that queries the Kubernetes API across two clusters,
   aggregates GPU utilization, and recommends which cluster should receive the
   next training job based on available capacity.
3. Write a Kustomize overlay structure (base + overlays) that customizes a
   PyTorch training job for on-prem (InfiniBand, local NVMe storage) vs. cloud
   (EFA, S3 FUSE mount) environments.

Deliver all three artifacts as working configuration files and scripts.
hints: Use clusterSelector.matchLabels in Fleet targets to differentiate cluster types
The kubernetes Python client supports multiple kubeconfig contexts — iterate over them
Kustomize patches can replace volume mounts, node selectors, and environment variables per overlay
exercises.solution: The Fleet GitRepo should define three targets with distinct label selectors (e.g., accelerator: nvidia-a100, provider: aws, role: cpu-worker). The Python aggregator loads multiple kubeconfig contexts, queries each cluster's node list for 'nvidia.com/gpu' capacity and allocatable values, computes utilization percentages, and returns the cluster with the lowest GPU utilization. The Kustomize structure uses a base/ directory with the core PyTorchJob spec, an overlays/onprem/ directory that patches in hostNetwork: true, InfiniBand device requests, and NVMe hostPath volumes, and an overlays/cloud/ directory that patches in EFA device plugin resources and S3 CSI driver PVCs.

lessons.title: Cost Optimization & FinOps
objectives: Implement spot and preemptible instance strategies for GPU training workloads
Right-size GPU resources using profiling data and utilization metrics
Design chargeback and showback models for shared AI/HPC platforms
Perform total cost of ownership analysis comparing on-prem vs. cloud GPU
lessons.content_md:
# Cost Optimization & FinOps for AI/HPC

GPU compute is the single largest cost center for AI organizations. A single 8xH100 node
on AWS (p5.48xlarge) costs approximately $98/hour on-demand — that is over $860,000 per
year if run continuously. Mastering FinOps (Financial Operations) practices specific to
AI/HPC workloads is essential for sustainable scaling.

## Spot & Preemptible Instance Strategies

Cloud providers offer unused GPU capacity at 60-90% discounts through spot (AWS),
preemptible (GCP), and low-priority (Azure) instances. The trade-off is that instances can
be reclaimed with as little as 30 seconds notice (GCP) or 2 minutes (AWS).

**Making Training Spot-Safe:**

The key is checkpoint-based fault tolerance. Modern frameworks provide built-in support:

```python
import torch
import torch.distributed.checkpoint as dcp
from torch.distributed.checkpoint import FileSystemWriter

class SpotAwareTrainer:
    def __init__(self, model, optimizer, checkpoint_dir, save_interval=100):
        self.model = model
        self.optimizer = optimizer
        self.checkpoint_dir = checkpoint_dir
        self.save_interval = save_interval
        self.global_step = 0
        self._register_preemption_handler()

    def _register_preemption_handler(self):
        """Register SIGTERM handler for spot preemption."""
        import signal
        signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, self._emergency_checkpoint)

    def _emergency_checkpoint(self, signum, frame):
        """Save checkpoint immediately on preemption signal."""
        print(f"SIGTERM received at step {self.global_step}, saving emergency checkpoint...")
        self._save_checkpoint(tag="emergency")
        raise SystemExit(0)

    def _save_checkpoint(self, tag=None):
        step_tag = tag or f"step_{self.global_step}"
        state_dict = {
            "model": self.model.state_dict(),
            "optimizer": self.optimizer.state_dict(),
            "global_step": self.global_step,
        }
        writer = FileSystemWriter(f"{self.checkpoint_dir}/{step_tag}")
        dcp.save(state_dict, writer)
        print(f"Checkpoint saved: {step_tag}")

    def train_step(self, batch):
        # ... forward, backward, optimizer step ...
        self.global_step += 1
        if self.global_step % self.save_interval == 0:
            self._save_checkpoint()
```

**Spot Instance Kubernetes Configuration:**

```yaml
# Karpenter provisioner for spot GPU nodes
apiVersion: karpenter.sh/v1beta1
kind: NodePool
metadata:
  name: gpu-spot-training
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        capacity-type: spot
        workload-type: training
    spec:
      requirements:
        - key: karpenter.sh/capacity-type
          operator: In
          values: ["spot"]
        - key: node.kubernetes.io/instance-type
          operator: In
          values: ["p4d.24xlarge", "p4de.24xlarge", "p5.48xlarge"]
        - key: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
          operator: In
          values: ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"]
      nodeClassRef:
        name: gpu-spot-class
  disruption:
    consolidationPolicy: WhenEmpty
    consolidateAfter: 60s
  limits:
    cpu: "2000"
    memory: 8000Gi
    nvidia.com/gpu: "256"
```

## Right-Sizing GPU Resources

GPU underutilization is rampant. Studies show average GPU utilization in ML clusters is
often below 40%. Right-sizing requires profiling actual workloads:

```bash
# Profile GPU utilization with DCGM over a training run
dcgmi dmon -e 203,204,210,1001,1002 -d 1000 | tee gpu_profile.log
# Fields: GPU_UTIL, MEM_COPY_UTIL, SM_ACTIVE, FP16_ACTIVE, TENSOR_ACTIVE

# Analyze utilization to decide GPU type
# If TENSOR_ACTIVE < 20% and MEM_COPY_UTIL < 30%, consider:
#   - Smaller GPU (A10G instead of A100)
#   - GPU sharing (MIG or time-slicing)
#   - Batch size increase to improve utilization
```

**GPU Right-Sizing Decision Matrix:**

```
TENSOR_ACTIVE  │  MEM_COPY_UTIL  │  Recommendation
───────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────
> 60%          │  > 50%          │  Correct GPU tier, well-utilized
> 60%          │  < 30%          │  Compute-bound — consider faster GPU
< 30%          │  > 50%          │  Memory-bound — increase batch size
< 30%          │  < 30%          │  Over-provisioned — downsize GPU tier
< 10%          │  < 10%          │  Severe waste — use MIG or CPU instead
```

## Chargeback & Showback Models

Shared GPU platforms need transparent cost attribution. Implement chargeback using
Kubernetes labels and resource metering:

```python
# GPU chargeback calculator using Prometheus metrics
import requests
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

PROM_URL = "http://prometheus:9090/api/v1"
GPU_HOUR_COST = {
    "nvidia-a100-80gb": 3.50,
    "nvidia-h100-80gb": 8.50,
    "nvidia-a10g": 1.20,
}

def get_team_gpu_cost(team: str, days: int = 30) -> dict:
    query = f"""
      sum by (gpu_model) (
        increase(
          DCGM_FI_PROF_GR_ENGINE_ACTIVE{{
            namespace=~"team-{team}-.*"
          }}[{days}d]
        )
      ) / 3600
    """
    resp = requests.get(f"{PROM_URL}/query", params={"query": query})
    results = resp.json()["data"]["result"]

    total_cost = 0.0
    breakdown = {}
    for r in results:
        gpu_model = r["metric"]["gpu_model"]
        gpu_hours = float(r["value"][1])
        cost = gpu_hours * GPU_HOUR_COST.get(gpu_model, 5.00)
        breakdown[gpu_model] = {"gpu_hours": round(gpu_hours, 1), "cost": round(cost, 2)}
        total_cost += cost

    return {"team": team, "period_days": days, "total_cost": round(total_cost, 2), "breakdown": breakdown}
```

## Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Analysis

When comparing on-prem vs. cloud, account for all cost components:

- **On-Prem:** Hardware (amortized 3-5yr), power, cooling, data center space, network,
  staff, spares inventory, and opportunity cost of procurement lead time (6-12 months).
- **Cloud:** Instance hours, egress, storage, managed services, and reserved instance
  commitments.

A common finding: on-prem wins at >60% sustained utilization over 3 years, while cloud
wins for bursty or experimental workloads. Most organizations land on a hybrid model where
a baseline on-prem footprint handles steady-state load and cloud handles peak demand.

exercises.title: Spot Instance Fault Tolerance
exercises.description: What is the PRIMARY mechanism that makes distributed GPU training jobs safe to run on spot/preemptible instances?
options: Using larger instance types so training completes before preemption
Periodic checkpointing with SIGTERM handlers for emergency saves on preemption
Running duplicate training jobs on on-demand instances as backup
Requesting spot instances in multiple availability zones simultaneously
exercises.correct_answer: Periodic checkpointing with SIGTERM handlers for emergency saves on preemption
hints: Spot instances can be reclaimed with only 2 minutes warning (AWS) or 30 seconds (GCP)
The key is preserving training state so the job can resume from where it left off
SIGTERM is the signal sent by cloud providers before terminating a spot instance
exercises.solution: Periodic checkpointing combined with SIGTERM-triggered emergency saves is the primary mechanism for spot-safe training. When a preemption signal arrives, the handler saves the current model weights, optimizer state, and training step to durable storage. The job can then resume from the last checkpoint on a new instance. Running duplicates wastes resources, larger instances don't prevent preemption, and multi-AZ helps availability but doesn't prevent data loss on termination.

exercises.title: GPU Utilization Analyzer and Cost Reporter
exercises.description:
Write a Python class `GpuCostAnalyzer` that:

1. Accepts a list of GPU usage records, each containing: team (str), gpu_model (str),
   gpu_hours (float), and utilization_pct (float, 0-100).
2. Implements a `compute_chargeback(self) -> dict` method that calculates cost per team
   using these rates: nvidia-a100-80gb=$3.50/hr, nvidia-h100-80gb=$8.50/hr,
   nvidia-a10g=$1.20/hr. Unknown models default to $5.00/hr.
3. Implements a `find_wasteful_allocations(self, threshold=30.0) -> list` that returns
   records where utilization_pct is below the threshold, sorted ascending by utilization.
4. Implements a `recommend_rightsizing(self) -> list[dict]` that suggests downsizing
   GPU tier when utilization < 30% (e.g., H100 -> A100, A100 -> A10G).

All monetary values should be rounded to 2 decimal places.
hints: Store the cost rates in a class-level dictionary for easy lookup
Use dict.get(gpu_model, 5.00) for the default rate
For rightsizing, define a downgrade mapping: {'nvidia-h100-80gb': 'nvidia-a100-80gb', 'nvidia-a100-80gb': 'nvidia-a10g'}

lessons.title: Security & Compliance
objectives: Implement RBAC policies for multi-tenant AI/HPC platforms
Configure network policies to isolate training workloads
Manage secrets securely for model registries, data stores, and API keys
Design compliance controls for SOC2 and HIPAA in ML platform environments
Establish data governance policies for training data and model artifacts
lessons.content_md:
# Security & Compliance for AI/HPC Platforms

Enterprise AI platforms handle sensitive data (PII, PHI, proprietary models) and consume
expensive GPU resources. A security breach can expose training data, leak proprietary model
weights, or allow cryptomining on GPU nodes. This lesson covers the security controls and
compliance frameworks required for production ML platforms.

## Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

Kubernetes RBAC should enforce least-privilege access to GPU namespaces. Define roles that
map to organizational responsibilities:

```yaml
# ClusterRole for ML Engineers — can submit and monitor training jobs
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: ml-engineer
rules:
  - apiGroups: ["kubeflow.org"]
    resources: ["pytorchjobs", "mpijobs", "tfjobs"]
    verbs: ["create", "get", "list", "watch", "delete"]
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods", "pods/log", "events"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["configmaps", "secrets"]
    verbs: ["get", "list"]
    # Note: no create/update on secrets — handled by platform team
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["persistentvolumeclaims"]
    verbs: ["create", "get", "list", "delete"]
---
# ClusterRole for Platform Admins — full GPU and node management
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: platform-admin
rules:
  - apiGroups: ["*"]
    resources: ["*"]
    verbs: ["*"]
  - nonResourceURLs: ["*"]
    verbs: ["*"]
---
# RoleBinding scoping ML engineer to their team namespace
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: alice-ml-engineer
  namespace: team-nlp
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice@company.com
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: ml-engineer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
```

## Network Policies

Training jobs often need access to data stores but should not reach other teams'
namespaces or the public internet (to prevent data exfiltration):

```yaml
# Deny all ingress/egress by default, then allow specific flows
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: training-isolation
  namespace: team-nlp
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      workload-type: training
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
  ingress:
    # Allow NCCL peer-to-peer between training pods in same job
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              workload-type: training
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 29400   # PyTorch distributed rendezvous
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 29500   # NCCL socket
  egress:
    # Allow DNS resolution
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
    # Allow access to internal object storage
    - to:
        - ipBlock:
            cidr: 10.0.0.0/8
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 443
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 9000   # MinIO
    # Block all public internet access
```

## Secrets Management

Never store credentials in ConfigMaps, environment variables in pod specs, or container
images. Use a secrets management solution:

```yaml
# External Secrets Operator — sync from HashiCorp Vault
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ExternalSecret
metadata:
  name: model-registry-creds
  namespace: team-nlp
spec:
  refreshInterval: 1h
  secretStoreRef:
    name: vault-backend
    kind: ClusterSecretStore
  target:
    name: model-registry-creds
    creationPolicy: Owner
  data:
    - secretKey: aws-access-key-id
      remoteRef:
        key: secret/data/ml-platform/s3
        property: access_key_id
    - secretKey: aws-secret-access-key
      remoteRef:
        key: secret/data/ml-platform/s3
        property: secret_access_key
```

## Container Image Security

Scan all training images for vulnerabilities before allowing them on GPU nodes:

```bash
# Trivy scan with severity threshold
trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL \
  --exit-code 1 \
  --ignore-unfixed \
  registry.internal/ml-team/training:v2.1.0

# Enforce via Kyverno admission policy
```

```yaml
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-image-scan
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-trivy-scan
      match:
        any:
          - resources:
              kinds: ["Pod"]
              namespaceSelector:
                matchLabels:
                  requires-scan: "true"
      verifyImages:
        - imageReferences: ["registry.internal/*"]
          attestors:
            - entries:
                - keys:
                    publicKeys: |-
                      -----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----
                      MFkwEwYHKoZIzj0CAQYIKoZIzj0DAQcDQgAE...
                      -----END PUBLIC KEY-----
```

## SOC2 & HIPAA Compliance

For ML platforms processing healthcare or financial data:

- **SOC2 CC6.1 (Logical Access):** Enforce RBAC + SSO (OIDC) + MFA for cluster access.
  Audit all kubectl and API server access via audit logging.
- **HIPAA §164.312(a)(1) (Access Control):** Namespace-level isolation with network
  policies. Encrypt data at rest (LUKS for local NVMe, SSE-S3 for object storage) and
  in transit (mTLS via service mesh).
- **Data Governance:** Tag datasets and models with classification labels. Use OPA/Gatekeeper
  policies to prevent PHI-classified data from being mounted in non-compliant namespaces.

Audit logging should capture all GPU resource requests and data access patterns:

```yaml
# Kubernetes audit policy for ML platform compliance
apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1
kind: Policy
rules:
  - level: RequestResponse
    resources:
      - group: "kubeflow.org"
        resources: ["pytorchjobs", "mpijobs"]
    namespaces: ["team-*"]
  - level: Metadata
    resources:
      - group: ""
        resources: ["secrets"]
  - level: None
    resources:
      - group: ""
        resources: ["configmaps"]
        resourceNames: ["kube-root-ca.crt"]
```

exercises.title: Network Policy for Training Isolation
exercises.description: In a Kubernetes NetworkPolicy that isolates distributed training pods, which port must be explicitly allowed in the INGRESS rules to prevent NCCL communication failures between workers?
options: Port 29500 (NCCL socket) for collective communication between workers
exercises.correct_answer: Port 29500 (NCCL socket) for collective communication between workers
hints: NCCL (NVIDIA Collective Communication Library) uses socket-based transport as a fallback
The default NCCL socket port is typically 29500, configured via NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME
Without this port open between training pods, all-reduce operations will hang or fail
exercises.solution: Port 29500 is the default NCCL socket interface port used for collective operations (all-reduce, all-gather, broadcast) between distributed training workers. When network policies block inter-pod traffic by default, this port must be explicitly allowed in ingress rules for pods with matching training labels. Port 443 is for external HTTPS; SSH (22) may be needed for MPI but not NCCL; and the Kubernetes API port (6443) is on the control plane, not between worker pods.

exercises.title: Secure Multi-Tenant GPU Platform Configuration
exercises.description:
Design a complete security configuration for a multi-tenant GPU platform with three
teams (nlp, vision, speech):

1. Write RBAC manifests (ClusterRole + RoleBindings) that give each team:
   - Full access to PyTorchJob/MPIJob resources in their own namespace
   - Read-only access to pods and logs
   - No access to secrets (managed by platform team)
2. Write a NetworkPolicy per namespace that:
   - Allows NCCL traffic (ports 29400-29500) between same-namespace training pods
   - Allows egress to internal object storage (10.0.50.0/24) on port 9000
   - Allows DNS (UDP 53) to kube-system
   - Denies all other ingress and egress
3. Write an ExternalSecret manifest that syncs Vault secrets for S3 credentials
   into each team namespace with automatic 1-hour rotation.
4. Write a Kyverno policy that blocks any pod in gpu-* namespaces from using
   images not prefixed with `registry.internal/`.

Validate that your RBAC does not accidentally grant cluster-wide permissions.
hints: Use RoleBinding (not ClusterRoleBinding) to scope permissions to specific namespaces
NetworkPolicy podSelector with matchLabels ensures only training pods get NCCL access
Kyverno's validate.pattern can match image strings with wildcards
ExternalSecret refreshInterval controls rotation frequency
exercises.solution: The RBAC configuration uses a single ClusterRole (ml-engineer) with rules for kubeflow.org resources (create, get, list, watch, delete), pods/logs (get, list, watch), and no secret access. Three RoleBindings (one per namespace: team-nlp, team-vision, team-speech) bind respective team groups to this role. The NetworkPolicy in each namespace sets policyTypes [Ingress, Egress], allows ingress from same-namespace pods on TCP 29400-29500, allows egress to 10.0.50.0/24:9000, DNS to kube-system on UDP 53, and has no other rules — effectively denying everything else. The ExternalSecret with refreshInterval: 1h syncs from vault-backend ClusterSecretStore, mapping keys like secret/data/teams/{team}/s3 to local secret names. The Kyverno ClusterPolicy uses a validate rule with pattern matching on spec.containers[*].image: "registry.internal/*" with validationFailureAction: Enforce, applied to namespaces matching label gpu-enabled: "true".

lessons.title: Performance Tuning at Scale
objectives: Configure NCCL environment variables to maximize distributed training throughput
Optimize InfiniBand and RDMA settings for GPU-to-GPU communication
Implement topology-aware scheduling for multi-node GPU workloads
Apply mixed precision training and Flash Attention for compute efficiency
Profile and diagnose performance bottlenecks in large-scale training jobs
lessons.content_md:
# Performance Tuning at Scale

Scaling from a single GPU to hundreds introduces performance bottlenecks at every layer:
interconnect bandwidth, collective communication patterns, memory hierarchy, and compute
efficiency. This lesson covers the critical tuning parameters and techniques that separate
a well-optimized distributed training pipeline from one that wastes 50% or more of its
theoretical throughput.

## NCCL Tuning Variables

NCCL (NVIDIA Collective Communication Library) is the backbone of distributed training on
NVIDIA GPUs. Its behavior is controlled entirely through environment variables:

```bash
# Essential NCCL environment variables for large-scale training
export NCCL_DEBUG=INFO                    # Enable debug logging (use WARN in production)
export NCCL_IB_DISABLE=0                  # Enable InfiniBand (set 1 to force TCP)
export NCCL_IB_HCA=mlx5                   # Specify InfiniBand HCA device prefix
export NCCL_IB_GID_INDEX=3                # RoCE GID index (for RoCE v2 over Ethernet)
export NCCL_IB_TIMEOUT=23                 # IB timeout (2^23 * 4.096us ≈ 34s)
export NCCL_IB_RETRY_CNT=7               # Retry count for IB operations

# Network and buffer tuning
export NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME=eth0            # Socket interface for OOB communication
export NCCL_BUFFSIZE=8388608              # 8MB ring buffer (default 4MB)
export NCCL_NTHREADS=512                  # Threads per NCCL block (256-512)
export NCCL_MAX_NCHANNELS=32              # Max channels for parallel comm
export NCCL_MIN_NCHANNELS=16              # Min channels to ensure parallelism

# Algorithm selection
export NCCL_ALGO=Ring,Tree                # Allow both Ring and Tree algorithms
export NCCL_PROTO=LL128,LL,Simple         # Protocol preference order

# Cross-node optimization
export NCCL_CROSS_NIC=1                   # Allow cross-NIC communication
export NCCL_NET_GDR_LEVEL=SYS             # GPUDirect RDMA level (PHB, SYS)
export NCCL_P2P_LEVEL=NVL                 # P2P via NVLink when available
```

The `NCCL_ALGO` variable is particularly important. For small messages, the **Tree**
algorithm achieves lower latency (O(log N) steps). For large tensors (gradient
all-reduce), the **Ring** algorithm achieves optimal bandwidth utilization. NCCL
auto-selects based on message size, but you can force a specific algorithm for
benchmarking.

## InfiniBand & RDMA Optimization

InfiniBand provides 200-400 Gb/s bandwidth with microsecond-level latency. Proper
configuration is essential:

```bash
# Verify InfiniBand link status and speed
ibstat mlx5_0 | grep -E "State|Rate|Port"
# Expected: State: Active, Rate: 200 (HDR) or 400 (NDR)

# Check RDMA device capabilities
ibv_devinfo -d mlx5_0 | grep -E "max_qp|max_cq|max_mr"

# Benchmark RDMA write bandwidth between two nodes
# Server:
ib_write_bw -d mlx5_0 --report_gbits -D 10
# Client:
ib_write_bw -d mlx5_0 --report_gbits -D 10 <server-ip>
# Expected: ~190 Gb/s for HDR, ~380 Gb/s for NDR

# Verify GPUDirect RDMA is active
nvidia-smi topo -m
# Look for "NV#" (NVLink) and "SYS" (cross-socket via PCIe/IB)
```

**GPUDirect RDMA** allows InfiniBand NICs to read/write GPU memory directly, bypassing
the CPU. This eliminates a memory copy and reduces latency by ~50%:

```
Without GPUDirect:     GPU ──► CPU RAM ──► NIC ──► Network ──► NIC ──► CPU RAM ──► GPU
With GPUDirect RDMA:   GPU ──────────────► NIC ──► Network ──► NIC ──────────────► GPU
```

## Topology-Aware Scheduling

GPU node topology matters enormously. An 8xH100 DGX node has NVLink within the node
(~900 GB/s bisection) but relies on InfiniBand between nodes (~50 GB/s). Scheduling
should co-locate communicating ranks:

```yaml
# Topology-aware scheduling with pod affinity
apiVersion: kubeflow.org/v1
kind: PyTorchJob
metadata:
  name: llm-training-70b
spec:
  elasticPolicy:
    rdzvBackend: etcd
    minReplicas: 4
    maxReplicas: 8
  pytorchReplicaSpecs:
    Worker:
      replicas: 8
      template:
        spec:
          affinity:
            podAntiAffinity:
              preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution: []
            podAffinity:
              requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
                - labelSelector:
                    matchExpressions:
                      - key: training.kubeflow.org/job-name
                        operator: In
                        values: ["llm-training-70b"]
                  topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/rack
            nodeAffinity:
              requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
                nodeSelectorTerms:
                  - matchExpressions:
                      - key: nvidia.com/gpu.product
                        operator: In
                        values: ["NVIDIA-H100-80GB-HBM3"]
          schedulerName: volcano
          containers:
            - name: worker
              image: registry.internal/llm-training:v3.2
              resources:
                limits:
                  nvidia.com/gpu: 8
                  rdma/rdma_shared_device_a: 1
              env:
                - name: NCCL_IB_HCA
                  value: "mlx5"
                - name: NCCL_TOPO_DUMP_FILE
                  value: "/var/run/nvidia/nccl-topo.xml"
```

## Mixed Precision & Flash Attention

Compute efficiency is as important as communication efficiency:

```python
import torch
from torch.cuda.amp import GradScaler
from flash_attn import flash_attn_func

# Mixed precision training with BF16 (preferred on H100/A100)
scaler = GradScaler(enabled=True)

for batch in dataloader:
    optimizer.zero_grad()

    with torch.autocast(device_type="cuda", dtype=torch.bfloat16):
        # Flash Attention v2 — O(N) memory instead of O(N^2)
        # Input shapes: (batch, seqlen, nheads, headdim)
        q = model.q_proj(batch["input_ids"])
        k = model.k_proj(batch["input_ids"])
        v = model.v_proj(batch["input_ids"])

        # Flash attention: 2-4x faster than standard attention
        attn_output = flash_attn_func(
            q, k, v,
            dropout_p=0.0 if not model.training else 0.1,
            softmax_scale=None,  # defaults to 1/sqrt(headdim)
            causal=True,         # autoregressive mask
        )

        loss = model.compute_loss(attn_output, batch["labels"])

    # Scale loss and backward pass in mixed precision
    scaler.scale(loss).backward()
    scaler.unscale_(optimizer)
    torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)
    scaler.step(optimizer)
    scaler.update()
```

**Flash Attention** reduces memory from O(N²) to O(N) and avoids materializing the full
attention matrix. On a 4096-token sequence with 32 heads, this saves ~2GB of activation
memory per layer and provides 2-4x wall-clock speedup.

**Performance Profiling Workflow:**

```bash
# 1. Profile with NVIDIA Nsight Systems
nsys profile -t cuda,nvtx -o training_profile \
  --capture-range=cudaProfilerApi \
  python train.py --profile-steps 10-20

# 2. Analyze the trace
nsys stats training_profile.nsys-rep --report cuda_gpu_trace

# 3. Check for communication bottlenecks
grep -E "ncclAllReduce|ncclBroadcast" training_profile.sqlite \
  | awk '{sum+=$NF; count++} END {print "Avg NCCL time:", sum/count, "ms"}'
```

exercises.title: NCCL Algorithm Selection
exercises.description: For a gradient all-reduce operation on large tensors (>256MB) across 64 GPUs, which NCCL algorithm provides the BEST bandwidth utilization?
options: Tree algorithm — it minimizes latency with O(log N) steps
Ring algorithm — it achieves near-optimal bandwidth with N-1/N efficiency
Recursive halving-doubling — it balances latency and bandwidth
Direct all-to-all — it sends data directly between all pairs
exercises.correct_answer: Ring algorithm — it achieves near-optimal bandwidth with N-1/N efficiency
hints: Large tensor transfers are bandwidth-bound, not latency-bound
The Ring algorithm divides data into N chunks and pipelines them through the ring
Ring achieves (N-1)/N bandwidth utilization, which approaches 100% as N grows
exercises.solution: For large tensors (>256MB), the Ring algorithm provides the best bandwidth utilization because it pipelines data in N-1 steps, each transferring 1/N of the total data. This achieves (N-1)/N ≈ 98.4% bandwidth efficiency with 64 GPUs. The Tree algorithm is better for small messages where latency (O(log N) vs O(N) steps) dominates. Recursive halving-doubling is a theoretical construct not directly used in NCCL. Direct all-to-all would create N² communication pairs and congest the network.

exercises.title: NCCL Environment Configuration Generator
exercises.description:
Write a Python function `generate_nccl_config` that produces an optimized set of NCCL
environment variables based on cluster hardware:

Parameters:
  - num_nodes (int): Number of nodes in the cluster
  - gpus_per_node (int): GPUs per node (typically 4 or 8)
  - interconnect (str): One of "infiniband_hdr", "infiniband_ndr", "roce", "tcp"
  - gpu_model (str): One of "a100", "h100", "a10g"

The function should return a dict[str, str] of environment variable names to values with
these rules:
  - If interconnect is infiniband_*, set NCCL_IB_DISABLE=0, NCCL_IB_HCA=mlx5,
    NCCL_NET_GDR_LEVEL=SYS, NCCL_IB_TIMEOUT=23, NCCL_IB_RETRY_CNT=7
  - If interconnect is "infiniband_ndr", additionally set NCCL_IB_QPS_PER_CONNECTION=4
  - If interconnect is "roce", set NCCL_IB_DISABLE=0, NCCL_IB_GID_INDEX=3,
    NCCL_IB_ROCE_ENABLE=1
  - If interconnect is "tcp", set NCCL_IB_DISABLE=1, NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME=eth0
  - Always set NCCL_BUFFSIZE=8388608 for >=16 total GPUs, else 4194304
  - Set NCCL_MAX_NCHANNELS to min(32, gpus_per_node * 4)
  - For h100 GPUs, set NCCL_P2P_LEVEL=NVL; for a100, set NCCL_P2P_LEVEL=SYS
  - Always include NCCL_DEBUG=WARN
hints: Start with a base config dict and conditionally update it based on parameters
Use str(value) to ensure all dict values are strings
interconnect.startswith('infiniband') catches both HDR and NDR variants
exercises.solution:
```python
def generate_nccl_config(
    num_nodes: int,
    gpus_per_node: int,
    interconnect: str,
    gpu_model: str,
) -> dict[str, str]:
    total_gpus = num_nodes * gpus_per_node
    config = {"NCCL_DEBUG": "WARN"}

    # Buffer size based on cluster scale
    config["NCCL_BUFFSIZE"] = str(8388608 if total_gpus >= 16 else 4194304)
    config["NCCL_MAX_NCHANNELS"] = str(min(32, gpus_per_node * 4))

    # Interconnect-specific settings
    if interconnect.startswith("infiniband"):
        config["NCCL_IB_DISABLE"] = "0"
        config["NCCL_IB_HCA"] = "mlx5"
        config["NCCL_NET_GDR_LEVEL"] = "SYS"
        config["NCCL_IB_TIMEOUT"] = "23"
        config["NCCL_IB_RETRY_CNT"] = "7"
        if interconnect == "infiniband_ndr":
            config["NCCL_IB_QPS_PER_CONNECTION"] = "4"
    elif interconnect == "roce":
        config["NCCL_IB_DISABLE"] = "0"
        config["NCCL_IB_GID_INDEX"] = "3"
        config["NCCL_IB_ROCE_ENABLE"] = "1"
    elif interconnect == "tcp":
        config["NCCL_IB_DISABLE"] = "1"
        config["NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME"] = "eth0"

    # GPU-specific P2P settings
    if gpu_model == "h100":
        config["NCCL_P2P_LEVEL"] = "NVL"
    elif gpu_model == "a100":
        config["NCCL_P2P_LEVEL"] = "SYS"

    return config
```

exercises.title: End-to-End Performance Profiling and Optimization
exercises.description:
Perform a complete performance tuning exercise for a distributed training workload:

1. Write a shell script that uses `nvidia-smi`, `dcgmi dmon`, and `ibstat` to collect
   baseline metrics: GPU utilization, memory bandwidth, tensor core activity, and
   InfiniBand link rate. Output results as a structured JSON report.
2. Create a PyTorchJob manifest for an 8-node, 64-GPU training job with:
   - Topology-aware pod affinity (same rack preferred)
   - Complete NCCL environment configuration for InfiniBand NDR
   - Resource requests including rdma/rdma_shared_device_a
   - Volcano scheduler with gang-scheduling (all 8 pods or none)
3. Write a Python profiling wrapper that:
   - Measures per-step training time (forward, backward, optimizer, NCCL)
   - Logs MFU (Model FLOPS Utilization) computed as actual_flops / peak_flops
   - Detects communication bottlenecks when NCCL time > 30% of step time
   - Outputs a summary CSV with columns: step, forward_ms, backward_ms,
     nccl_ms, optimizer_ms, total_ms, mfu_pct

Target: achieve >50% MFU on a hypothetical 64-GPU H100 cluster.
hints: Use nvidia-smi --query-gpu=utilization.gpu,utilization.memory,memory.used --format=csv for structured GPU data
DCGM field IDs: 203=GPU_UTIL, 1001=FP16_ACTIVE, 1002=TENSOR_ACTIVE
H100 SXM peak BF16 is ~990 TFLOPS; MFU = measured_tflops / (num_gpus * 990)
Wrap torch.cuda.Event with synchronize() for accurate per-phase timing
exercises.solution: The shell script uses nvidia-smi CSV output and ibstat parsed with awk, outputting JSON via jq. The PyTorchJob spec includes podAffinity with topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/rack, nodeAffinity for H100 nodes, env vars for NCCL_IB_HCA=mlx5, NCCL_NET_GDR_LEVEL=SYS, NCCL_IB_QPS_PER_CONNECTION=4, NCCL_BUFFSIZE=8388608, and schedulerName: volcano with a PodGroup annotation for minMember: 8. The Python profiler creates torch.cuda.Events around each phase (forward, backward, NCCL, optimizer), calls synchronize, and computes elapsed_time_ms. MFU is calculated as (model_flops_per_step * steps_per_second) / (64 * 990e12) * 100. When nccl_ms/total_ms > 0.3, it logs a warning suggesting gradient compression or increasing NCCL channels.

lessons.title: Platform Architecture Design
objectives: Design reference architectures for enterprise AI/HPC platforms
Perform capacity planning for GPU clusters based on workload projections
Architect disaster recovery and business continuity for ML platforms
Analyze real-world platform designs from Meta, Google, and Microsoft
lessons.content_md:
# Platform Architecture Design

Designing an AI/HPC platform that serves hundreds of researchers, handles petabytes of
data, and manages thousands of GPUs requires thinking across every layer of the stack.
This lesson synthesizes the course material into cohesive reference architectures and
studies how the world's leading AI organizations design their platforms.

## Reference Architecture: Enterprise AI Platform

A production AI platform consists of five layers:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        User Interface Layer                        │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐   │
│  │ JupyterHub│  │ MLflow UI    │  │ Grafana  │  │ CLI / SDK    │   │
│  └──────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────────┘   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                     Workflow & Orchestration                        │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐   │
│  │ Kubeflow │  │ Argo Workflows│  │ Airflow  │  │ Ray Clusters │   │
│  │ Training │  │  Pipelines   │  │  DAGs    │  │  (Serve/Tune)│   │
│  └──────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────────┘   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                    Resource Management Layer                        │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐   │
│  │Kubernetes│  │   Volcano    │  │ Karpenter│  │ GPU Operator │   │
│  │  (EKS/   │  │  Gang Sched  │  │ Autoscale│  │ MIG / MPS   │   │
│  │  GKE)    │  │  Fair Share  │  │          │  │              │   │
│  └──────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────────┘   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                      Data & Storage Layer                          │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐   │
│  │ S3/GCS   │  │ Lustre/GPFS  │  │ NVMe     │  │ Feature Store│   │
│  │ (bulk)   │  │ (parallel)   │  │ (local)  │  │ (Feast)      │   │
│  └──────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────────┘   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                     Infrastructure Layer                            │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐   │
│  │ DGX/HGX  │  │ InfiniBand   │  │ Power &  │  │ BMC/Redfish  │   │
│  │ GPU Nodes│  │ Fabric       │  │ Cooling  │  │ Management   │   │
│  └──────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────────┘   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Capacity Planning

GPU capacity planning must account for multiple workload types with different resource
profiles and scheduling characteristics:

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List

@dataclass
class WorkloadProfile:
    name: str
    gpu_count: int           # GPUs per job
    avg_duration_hours: float
    jobs_per_week: int
    priority: int            # 1=highest

@dataclass
class CapacityPlan:
    total_gpu_hours_weekly: float
    peak_concurrent_gpus: int
    recommended_gpus: int
    utilization_target: float

def plan_capacity(
    workloads: List[WorkloadProfile],
    target_utilization: float = 0.70,
    peak_to_avg_ratio: float = 2.5,
) -> CapacityPlan:
    """Compute required GPU capacity from workload projections."""
    total_gpu_hours = sum(
        w.gpu_count * w.avg_duration_hours * w.jobs_per_week
        for w in workloads
    )
    # Average concurrent GPUs needed
    avg_concurrent = total_gpu_hours / (7 * 24)  # hours in a week
    # Peak estimate using multiplier
    peak_concurrent = int(avg_concurrent * peak_to_avg_ratio)
    # Recommended capacity accounts for target utilization headroom
    recommended = int(peak_concurrent / target_utilization)
    # Round up to nearest node boundary (8 GPUs per node)
    recommended = ((recommended + 7) // 8) * 8

    return CapacityPlan(
        total_gpu_hours_weekly=round(total_gpu_hours, 1),
        peak_concurrent_gpus=peak_concurrent,
        recommended_gpus=recommended,
        utilization_target=target_utilization,
    )

# Example: plan for a mid-size AI org
workloads = [
    WorkloadProfile("LLM Pre-training", 64, 72.0, 2, priority=1),
    WorkloadProfile("Fine-tuning", 8, 4.0, 50, priority=2),
    WorkloadProfile("Inference Dev", 1, 2.0, 200, priority=3),
    WorkloadProfile("Hyperparameter Sweep", 4, 6.0, 30, priority=2),
]
plan = plan_capacity(workloads)
# total_gpu_hours_weekly ≈ 9,920 + 1,600 + 400 + 720 = 12,640
# avg_concurrent ≈ 75.2 GPUs
# peak_concurrent ≈ 188 GPUs
# recommended ≈ 272 GPUs (34 nodes × 8 GPUs)
```

## Disaster Recovery for ML Platforms

ML platform DR differs from traditional application DR because of the unique assets:

- **Training State:** Active training jobs cannot be trivially migrated. Checkpoint to
  durable storage (S3, GCS) every N steps. Recovery means restarting from the last
  checkpoint — plan for lost compute time.
- **Model Registry:** MLflow, Weights & Biases, or custom registries contain irreplaceable
  model artifacts. Replicate across regions with object storage replication.
- **Training Data:** Often terabytes to petabytes. Use cross-region replication for
  critical datasets. Accept that full dataset recovery may take days — prioritize the
  most active datasets.

**DR Tier Classification:**

```
Tier  │  Asset               │  RPO      │  RTO       │  Strategy
──────┼──────────────────────┼───────────┼────────────┼────────────────────
1     │  Model Registry      │  1 hour   │  4 hours   │  Cross-region replication
2     │  Training Checkpoints│  1 step   │  30 min    │  Multi-region object store
3     │  Training Data       │  24 hours │  48 hours  │  Async replication
4     │  Dev Environments    │  7 days   │  24 hours  │  GitOps rebuild
```

## Case Studies

**Meta's Research SuperCluster (RSC):**
Meta built RSC with 2,000 A100 GPUs connected via InfiniBand NDR. Key design choices:
- Fully air-gapped network for security (no internet access from GPU nodes)
- Custom job scheduler integrated with their FairSeq training framework
- Lustre parallel file system for training data at 16 TB/s aggregate bandwidth
- Designed for models with >1 trillion parameters using 4D parallelism

**Google's TPU Pods and Pathways:**
Google's approach uses custom TPU accelerators in pod configurations (up to 4,096 TPU v5p
chips). The Pathways system enables a single training job to span multiple TPU pods with
heterogeneous parallelism strategies. Key insight: tight integration between hardware,
compiler (XLA), and framework (JAX) enables optimizations impossible on commodity hardware.

**Microsoft Azure's AI Infrastructure:**
Azure's ND-series VMs use custom InfiniBand fabrics. Their Singularity platform implements
global scheduling across all GPU clusters worldwide, enabling preemptive job placement
based on real-time availability. Key innovation: transparent checkpoint-resume that allows
jobs to be migrated between clusters without user intervention — NCCL state is serialized
and restored on the target cluster.

These case studies share common themes: invest in high-bandwidth interconnects, build
fault tolerance into every layer, use custom scheduling for GPU-specific constraints, and
treat the platform as a product with SLAs for internal customers.

exercises.title: Enterprise AI Platform Architecture Design Document
exercises.description:
Design a complete AI/HPC platform for a hypothetical company with these requirements:

- 500 ML engineers across 10 teams
- Workloads: LLM pre-training (up to 256 GPUs), fine-tuning, inference, notebooks
- Budget: $15M annual GPU compute budget
- Compliance: SOC2 Type II required
- Availability: 99.9% platform uptime SLA
- Hybrid: 60% on-prem, 40% cloud burst capacity

Deliverables:
1. Architecture diagram (ASCII or description) showing all five layers from the
   reference architecture, with specific technology choices justified.
2. Capacity plan: compute total GPU-hours, recommend cluster size (on-prem + cloud),
   and specify node types (DGX H100 vs. cloud instances).
3. Networking design: InfiniBand topology for on-prem, cloud interconnect for burst,
   and hybrid connectivity (VPN/Direct Connect).
4. Storage architecture: tiered storage with parallel FS, object store, and local NVMe.
   Include bandwidth calculations for a 256-GPU training job reading 50TB dataset.
5. DR plan with RPO/RTO for each asset tier.
6. Cost breakdown: on-prem capex (amortized), cloud opex, networking, storage, staff.
7. 12-month implementation roadmap with phased delivery.

This should be a production-quality architecture document (3000+ words).
hints: $15M/year budget: A DGX H100 system (8xH100) costs ~$300K. That buys ~30 nodes (240 GPUs) on-prem with remaining budget for cloud, storage, networking, and staff
For 256-GPU training, you need InfiniBand NDR with fat-tree topology (non-blocking)
SOC2 requires documented access controls, audit logging, encryption, and incident response procedures
99.9% uptime = ~8.7 hours downtime/year. Plan for rolling upgrades and node failure tolerance
Lustre parallel FS can deliver ~1 TB/s aggregate; 256 GPUs each reading at 3 GB/s need 768 GB/s
exercises.solution: A strong architecture document would include: Infrastructure Layer — 30 DGX H100 nodes (240 on-prem GPUs) with InfiniBand NDR400 in a 2-level fat-tree topology, plus Karpenter-managed cloud burst to p5.48xlarge spot instances up to 160 cloud GPUs. Resource Management — Kubernetes (RKE2 on-prem, EKS for cloud) with Volcano scheduler for gang scheduling, Kueue for fair-share queuing across 10 teams. Storage — Lustre (DDN EXAScaler) with 1.2 TB/s aggregate for training, S3-compatible object store (MinIO) for checkpoints/models, local NVMe scratch on each node. Networking — NDR400 InfiniBand with SHARP in-network reduction, AWS Direct Connect 100G for cloud burst, Tailscale mesh for management. Security — OIDC with Okta, namespace-per-team RBAC, Kyverno admission policies, Trivy scanning, Vault for secrets. DR — Model registry replicated to S3 cross-region (RPO 1hr), checkpoints on multi-region object store, GitOps for platform rebuild (RTO 4hr). Cost — $9M on-prem capex amortized over 3yr ($3M/yr), $7.2M cloud, $2.4M storage/networking, $2.4M staff (8 platform engineers) = $15M total. Roadmap — Months 1-3: procurement, DC prep, Kubernetes setup; Months 4-6: storage, networking, security; Months 7-9: onboard first 3 teams, iterate; Months 10-12: full production, cloud burst, DR testing.

exercises.title: Platform Migration & Modernization Plan
exercises.description:
A company is migrating from a legacy HPC environment to a modern Kubernetes-based AI
platform. Current state:

- 50 bare-metal GPU servers (8x V100 each = 400 GPUs) managed by Slurm
- Lustre parallel file system (500 TB, 200 GB/s)
- 200 users submitting jobs via sbatch scripts
- No container usage; all dependencies installed via environment modules
- Single-tenant — no namespace isolation
- No GPU monitoring or chargeback

Design the migration plan:

1. Target state architecture on Kubernetes with backward compatibility for
   existing Slurm users during transition (consider Slurm-on-K8s bridges like
   Sunk or Slurm-GKE).
2. Containerization strategy: how to convert environment modules to container
   images without disrupting researchers. Include a Dockerfile template for common
   ML frameworks and a migration guide.
3. Storage migration: how to integrate existing Lustre with Kubernetes (CSI drivers,
   mount strategies) while adding object storage for cloud readiness.
4. Monitoring stack design: Prometheus + DCGM exporter + Grafana dashboards for
   GPU utilization, job metrics, and per-team chargeback.
5. User onboarding plan: phased migration of 200 users in cohorts, with training
   materials, office hours, and a compatibility layer that accepts sbatch-like
   commands but translates to Kubernetes jobs.
6. Risk register: top 10 migration risks with mitigation strategies.
7. Success metrics: KPIs to measure migration success (GPU utilization improvement,
   user satisfaction, time-to-first-GPU, job queue wait time).

Deliver as a structured migration plan with timelines and decision points.
hints: A Slurm-to-K8s bridge lets users keep using sbatch while jobs actually run on Kubernetes — this is critical for adoption
Environment modules can be mapped to container tags: 'module load pytorch/2.1' becomes 'image: ml-base:pytorch-2.1'
Lustre CSI driver (DDN Lustre CSI) mounts existing Lustre into Kubernetes pods with ReadWriteMany access
DCGM exporter exposes GPU metrics as Prometheus endpoints — deploy as a DaemonSet
Phase users by team: start with the most technically advanced team, get feedback, then expand
exercises.solution: The migration plan phases over 9 months. Phase 1 (Months 1-2): Deploy Kubernetes alongside existing Slurm (parallel operation). Install Lustre CSI driver so both systems access the same data. Deploy monitoring stack (Prometheus, DCGM exporter, Grafana). Phase 2 (Months 3-4): Build container images matching current environment modules using multi-stage Dockerfiles (base CUDA image + framework layers). Deploy sbatch compatibility wrapper (a CLI tool that parses sbatch scripts and generates equivalent Kubernetes Job manifests). Migrate first cohort (power users, 20 people) with dedicated support. Phase 3 (Months 5-6): Introduce namespaces and RBAC per team. Enable GPU chargeback using DCGM metrics. Add object storage (MinIO) alongside Lustre. Migrate second and third cohorts (80 people). Phase 4 (Months 7-9): Migrate remaining users. Decommission Slurm. Implement advanced features (Volcano scheduling, Kueue fair-share, autoscaling). Risk register includes: data loss during storage changes (mitigate: read-only Lustre period), researcher productivity loss (mitigate: parallel operation, compatibility layer), GPU underutilization during transition (mitigate: phased approach with monitoring), skill gap (mitigate: training program). Success KPIs: GPU utilization >60% (up from ~35%), median queue wait <15 min, user NPS >30, time-to-first-GPU <5 min for interactive jobs.
